paper_metadata.title: Understanding the resistance to carbon taxes: Drivers and barriers among the general public and fuel-tax protesters
paper_metadata.authors: Jens Ewald, Thomas Sterner, Erik Sterner
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Swedish national online panel and self-selected fuel-tax protest sample
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Swedish survey compares the general public and fuel-tax protesters on carbon-tax effectiveness, fairness, and revenue-use preferences, identifying trust, ideology, residence, education, and Pigouvian beliefs as key correlates.

determinants.definition: Indicator for respondents drawn from the Petrol Protest 2.0 fuel-tax protest movement.
determinants.mechanism: Captures organized opposition and protest identity associated with stronger resistance to carbon taxation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax attitudes in Sweden; comparison between the national public sample and fuel-tax protesters.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.evidence_quote: being a member of the protest movement is associated with a 19% decrease
determinants.notes: Sample-group indicator, not a causal treatment.

determinants.definition: Ordinal education categories from comprehensive schooling to master’s degree.
determinants.mechanism: More education may improve understanding of carbon-pricing incentives and policy trade-offs.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Swedish carbon-tax survey of public and protest samples.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.evidence_quote: Support for carbon taxation is positively correlated with education.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Master’s degree: +0.14*** effectiveness; +0.13*** ranking; +0.14*** collecting revenue.
determinants.notes: Higher education shows the strongest positive effects.

determinants.mechanism: Proxy for cost exposure, but no clear association with carbon-tax attitudes in this study.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.evidence_quote: household income does not appear to matter
determinants.notes: Null result; included because explicitly discussed.

determinants.definition: Self-reported area of residence, contrasted with city, town, village, and countryside.
determinants.mechanism: Rural households may depend more on cars and perceive fuel/carbon taxes as less fair.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.evidence_quote: urban residence ... low levels of support
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Countryside vs city: -0.13*** effectiveness; -0.10*** ranking; -0.15*** collecting revenue.
determinants.notes: Rural effect is strong and robust.

determinants.definition: Self-reported trust versus no trust in government.
determinants.mechanism: Low institutional trust reduces credibility that carbon-tax revenues will be used appropriately.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.evidence_quote: lack of trust in government ... correlate with opinions on carbon taxes
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: No trust: -0.23*** effectiveness; -0.17*** ranking; -0.24*** collecting revenue.
determinants.notes: Largest and most consistent correlate; stronger in protesters.

determinants.definition: Left categories on the self-placement left-right scale versus the centrist reference.
determinants.mechanism: Left-leaning respondents are more favorable to state intervention and carbon pricing.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Clearly left: +0.12*** effectiveness; +0.11*** ranking; +0.14*** collecting revenue.
determinants.notes: Left categories are more supportive than centrists.

determinants.definition: Right categories on the self-placement left-right scale versus the centrist reference.
determinants.mechanism: Right-leaning respondents are less favorable to taxation and carbon-pricing instruments.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Clearly right: -0.08*** effectiveness; -0.07*** ranking; -0.13*** collecting revenue.
determinants.notes: Right categories are less supportive than centrists.

determinants.definition: Belief that carbon taxation changes behavior and reduces fossil-fuel use.
determinants.mechanism: If respondents think the tax works, they are more likely to accept it and its revenue use.
determinants.outcome_variable: Carbon tax effectiveness, policy ranking, and support for using carbon-tax revenue.
determinants.evidence_quote: those who understand the Pigouvian mechanism are the most satisfied
determinants.notes: Key acceptance driver, especially among protesters.
